The shrine is featured in the {{ill|Edo meisho zue|en|Edo meisho zue|WD=Q2704637|lt=Edo meisho zue|1=Edo meisho zue}}.{{sfn|Edo Meisho Zue|1927|p=521}} == Overview == Surrounded by old and large trees, the shrine is situated in a serene environment teeming with various seasonal flora and fauna. Though typically tranquil, it attracts many visitors during {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} (New Year’s visits) and {{ill|Shichi-Go-San|en|Shichi-Go-San|WD=Q1072387|lt=Shichi-Go-San|1=Shichi-Go-San}} celebrations in November. During the nights of {{ill|Tori-no-Ichi|ja|酉の市|1=Tori-no-Ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|qq=}}, fireworks are displayed. The shrine also preserves records of mathematical tablets ({{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku|1=Sangaku}}) dedicated by local Edo-period mathematicians. Among these, the "Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Sangaku," donated by Kanesugi Kiyotoki’s disciples in 1833, is registered as a tangible cultural property of Adachi Ward.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.adachi.tokyo.jp/bunka/chiikibunka/bunkazai_ootorijinjasangaku.html|title=Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Sangaku|publisher=Adachi Ward|accessdate=2022-08-22}} == History == === Origins === The shrine's founding is attributed to the {{ill|Hōji (era)|en|Hōji (era)|WD=Q1327400|lt=Hōji|1=Hōji (era)}} period (1247–1249).{{sfn|Adachi Historical Society|1978|p=231}} It is said that during the {{ill|Gosannen War|en|Gosannen War|WD=Q1187411|lt=Later Three-Year War|1=Gosannen War}}, the Heian-period military commander {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|WD=Q3083118|lt=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu}} prayed for victory at this location after witnessing an eagle. This marked the shrine’s establishment.{{sfn|Adachi Historical Society|1992|p=162}} Initially referred to as {{ill|Ōtori Daimyōjin|ja|鷲明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the shrine was renamed Ōtori Shrine following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} during the Meiji era. * The shrine is mentioned in a {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōda|qq=}} dated to the first year of {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan’ei|1=Kan'ei}} (1624), indicating its existence prior to this date. * The oldest extant stone artifacts date back to the second year of {{ill|Manji (era)|en|Manji (era)|WD=Q1145295|lt=Manji|1=Manji (era)}} (1659). * During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} period, the shrine’s affiliated temple was {{ill|Shōkaku-in|ja|正覚院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkaku-in|1=Shogakuin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545644}}. * The current shrine structure dates back to the Ansei era, with contributions from the {{ill|Kubota Domain|en|Kubota Domain|WD=Q1067720|lt=Kubota Domain|1=Kubota Domain}}’s Satake family. Its wooden columns feature carvings attributed to a disciple of {{ill|Hidari Jingorō|en|Hidari Jingorō|WD=Q2668220|lt=Hidari Jingorō|1=Hidari Jingorō}}. === deity === While {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} is listed as the shrine’s current deity, historical records suggest that {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|WD=Q3083118|lt=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu}} was likely its original object of worship. The shrine received significant patronage from the Satake family and other clans claiming descent from Yoshimitsu. == Events == * '''Third Sunday of July''' ({{ill|Marine Day|en|Marine Day|WD=Q1355833|lt=Marine Day|1=Marine Day}}): {{ill|Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Lion Dance|ja|花畑大鷲神社獅子舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Lion Dance|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (designated an intangible cultural property of Adachi Ward). The shrine’s {{ill|Three-Lion Dance|ja|三匹獅子舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-Lion Dance|qq=}} is performed.{{sfn|Adachi Historical Society|1978|p=232}} * {{ill|Tori-no-Ichi|ja|酉の市|1=Tori-no-Ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|qq=}} == Access == * A 27-minute walk from {{ill|Yatsuka Station|en|Yatsuka Station|WD=Q8050280|lt=Yatsuka Station|1=Yatsuka Station}} on the {{ill|Tobu Isesaki Line|en|Tobu Isesaki Line|WD=Q906598|lt=Tōbu Isesaki Line|1=Tobu Isesaki Line}} ({{OSM route map|start=35.81411,139.80140|goal=35.80924,139.82021|name=Route}}). == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Adachi Historical Society. *History of Adachi Ward*. Meicho Publishing, 1978, pp. 230–232. * Adachi Historical Society (ed.). *Adachi Ward Historical Walks*. Gakuseisha, 1992, pp. 162–166. * {{cite book|title={{ill|Edo meisho zue|en|Edo meisho zue|WD=Q2704637|lt=Edo Meisho Zue|1=Edo meisho zue}}|volume=3|author=Yukio Saitō|publisher=Yūhōdō Shoten|year=1927|chapter=巻之六 開陽之部 正一位大鷲大明神社|pages=521–524|id={{NDLJP|1174157/265}}|ref={{sfnref|Edo Meisho Zue|1927}}}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Ōtori Shrine|ja|鷲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōtori taisha|en|Ōtori taisha|WD=Q705151|lt=Ōtori taisha|1=Ōtori taisha}} == External Links == * [http://ootori-jinja.or.jp/ Official Ōtori Shrine Website] {{Ōtori Faith}} {{translated page|ja|大鷲神社 (足立区)|version=103732014|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:大鷲神社 (足立区)]] [[ja:大鷲神社]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-jinja (Hanahata, Adachi)}} {{wikidata link|Q11442008}} [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Adachi, Tokyo]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:pages]]

The shrine received significant patronage from the Satake family and other clans claiming descent from Yoshimitsu. == Events == * '''Third Sunday of July''' ({{ill|Marine Day|en|Marine Day|WD=Q1355833|lt=Marine Day|1=Marine Day}}): {{ill|Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Lion Dance|ja|花畑大鷲神社獅子舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Lion Dance|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (designated an intangible cultural property of Adachi Ward). The shrine’s {{ill|Three-Lion Dance|ja|三匹獅子舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-Lion Dance|qq=}} is performed.{{sfn|Adachi Historical Society|1978|p=232}} * {{ill|Tori-no-Ichi|ja|酉の市|1=Tori-no-Ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|qq=}} == Access == * A 27-minute walk from {{ill|Yatsuka Station|en|Yatsuka Station|WD=Q8050280|lt=Yatsuka Station|1=Yatsuka Station}} on the {{ill|Tobu Isesaki Line|en|Tobu Isesaki Line|WD=Q906598|lt=Tōbu Isesaki Line|1=Tobu Isesaki Line}} ({{OSM route map|start=35.81411,139.80140|goal=35.80924,139.82021|name=Route}}). == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Adachi Historical Society. *History of Adachi Ward*. Meicho Publishing, 1978, pp. 230–232. * Adachi Historical Society (ed.). *Adachi Ward Historical Walks*. Gakuseisha, 1992, pp. 162–166. * {{cite book|title={{ill|Edo meisho zue|en|Edo meisho zue|WD=Q2704637|lt=Edo Meisho Zue|1=Edo meisho zue}}|volume=3|author=Yukio Saitō|publisher=Yūhōdō Shoten|year=1927|chapter=巻之六 開陽之部 正一位大鷲大明神社|pages=521–524|id={{NDLJP|1174157/265}}|ref={{sfnref|Edo Meisho Zue|1927}}}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Ōtori Shrine|ja|鷲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōtori taisha|en|Ōtori taisha|WD=Q705151|lt=Ōtori taisha|1=Ōtori taisha}} == External Links == * [http://ootori-jinja.or.jp/ Official Ōtori Shrine Website] {{Ōtori Faith}} {{translated page|ja|大鷲神社 (足立区)|version=103732014|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:大鷲神社 (足立区)]] [[ja:大鷲神社]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-jinja (Hanahata, Adachi)}} {{wikidata link|Q11442008}} [[Category:Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Adachi, Tokyo]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Ōtori-jinja (Hanahata, Adachi)]][[ja:大鷲神社 (足立区)]] [[co:commons:Category:Ōtori-jinja (Hanahata, Adachi)]][[ja:大鷲神社 (足立区)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ōtori-jinja (Hanahata, Adachi)]][[ja:大鷲神社 (足立区)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtori Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Ootori jinja hanahata adachi 2014.jpg | location = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Adachi, Tokyo|en|Adachi, Tokyo|WD=Q213464|lt=Adachi|1=Adachi, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Hanahata (Adachi)|ja|花畑 (足立区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanahata (Adachi)|1=Hanahata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615595}} 7-15-1 | Latitude = 35 | Latitude_min = 48 | Latitude_sec = 33.5 | Longitude = 139 | Longitude_min = 49 | Longitude_sec = 13 | Label_position = bottom | Map = Tokyo city | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|1=Yamato Takeru}} | Establishment = {{ill|Hōji (era)|en|Hōji (era)|WD=Q1327400|lt=Hōji|1=Hōji (era)}} period (1247–1249) | Main_building = | Alternate_names = Hanahata Ōtori Shrine | Festival = | Ceremonies = {{ill|Three-Lion Dance|ja|三匹獅子舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-Lion Dance|qq=}}
{{ill|Tori-no-Ichi|ja|酉の市|1=Tori-no-Ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|qq=}} | location = {{ill|Hanahata|WD=Q11615595|1=Hanahata}} | coordinates = {{Coord|35.79930278|N|139.81150556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Ōtori Shrine''' (大鷲神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Adachi, Tokyo|en|Adachi, Tokyo|WD=Q213464|lt=Adachi|1=Adachi, Tokyo}}, Tokyo. It serves as the {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}} (guardian deity) of the Hanahata area and is also known as '''Hanahata Ōtori Shrine''' (花畑大鷲神社). It is considered the origin of the well-known {{ill|Tori-no-Ichi|ja|酉の市|1=Tori-no-Ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|qq=}} markets, with its head shrine being {{ill|Ōtori taisha|en|Ōtori taisha|WD=Q705151|lt=Ōtori taisha|1=Ōtori taisha}}. The shrine is featured in the {{ill|Edo meisho zue|en|Edo meisho zue|WD=Q2704637|lt=Edo meisho zue|1=Edo meisho zue}}.{{sfn|Edo Meisho Zue|1927|p=521}} == Overview == Surrounded by old and large trees, the shrine is situated in a serene environment teeming with various seasonal flora and fauna. Though typically tranquil, it attracts many visitors during {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} (New Year’s visits) and {{ill|Shichi-Go-San|en|Shichi-Go-San|WD=Q1072387|lt=Shichi-Go-San|1=Shichi-Go-San}} celebrations in November. During the nights of {{ill|Tori-no-Ichi|ja|酉の市|1=Tori-no-Ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|qq=}}, fireworks are displayed. The shrine also preserves records of mathematical tablets ({{ill|Sangaku|en|Sangaku|WD=Q2569069|lt=Sangaku|1=Sangaku}}) dedicated by local Edo-period mathematicians. Among these, the "Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Sangaku," donated by Kanesugi Kiyotoki’s disciples in 1833, is registered as a tangible cultural property of Adachi Ward.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.adachi.tokyo.jp/bunka/chiikibunka/bunkazai_ootorijinjasangaku.html|title=Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Sangaku|publisher=Adachi Ward|accessdate=2022-08-22}} == History == === Origins === The shrine's founding is attributed to the {{ill|Hōji (era)|en|Hōji (era)|WD=Q1327400|lt=Hōji|1=Hōji (era)}} period (1247–1249).{{sfn|Adachi Historical Society|1978|p=231}} It is said that during the {{ill|Gosannen War|en|Gosannen War|WD=Q1187411|lt=Later Three-Year War|1=Gosannen War}}, the Heian-period military commander {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|WD=Q3083118|lt=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu}} prayed for victory at this location after witnessing an eagle. This marked the shrine’s establishment.{{sfn|Adachi Historical Society|1992|p=162}} Initially referred to as {{ill|Ōtori Daimyōjin|ja|鷲明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the shrine was renamed Ōtori Shrine following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} during the Meiji era. * The shrine is mentioned in a {{ill|Munafuda|ja|棟札|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōda|qq=}} dated to the first year of {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan’ei|1=Kan'ei}} (1624), indicating its existence prior to this date. * The oldest extant stone artifacts date back to the second year of {{ill|Manji (era)|en|Manji (era)|WD=Q1145295|lt=Manji|1=Manji (era)}} (1659). * During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} period, the shrine’s affiliated temple was {{ill|Shōkaku-in|ja|正覚院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkaku-in|1=Shogakuin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545644}}. * The current shrine structure dates back to the Ansei era, with contributions from the {{ill|Kubota Domain|en|Kubota Domain|WD=Q1067720|lt=Kubota Domain|1=Kubota Domain}}’s Satake family. Its wooden columns feature carvings attributed to a disciple of {{ill|Hidari Jingorō|en|Hidari Jingorō|WD=Q2668220|lt=Hidari Jingorō|1=Hidari Jingorō}}. === deity === While {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} is listed as the shrine’s current deity, historical records suggest that {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|WD=Q3083118|lt=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yoshimitsu}} was likely its original object of worship. The shrine received significant patronage from the Satake family and other clans claiming descent from Yoshimitsu. == Events == * '''Third Sunday of July''' ({{ill|Marine Day|en|Marine Day|WD=Q1355833|lt=Marine Day|1=Marine Day}}): {{ill|Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Lion Dance|ja|花畑大鷲神社獅子舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanahata Ōtori Shrine Lion Dance|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (designated an intangible cultural property of Adachi Ward). The shrine’s {{ill|Three-Lion Dance|ja|三匹獅子舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-Lion Dance|qq=}} is performed.{{sfn|Adachi Historical Society|1978|p=232}} * {{ill|Tori-no-Ichi|ja|酉の市|1=Tori-no-Ichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|13=User:Immanuelle/Tori-no-Ichi|qq=}} == Access == * A 27-minute walk from {{ill|Yatsuka Station|en|Yatsuka Station|WD=Q8050280|lt=Yatsuka Station|1=Yatsuka Station}} on the {{ill|Tobu Isesaki Line|en|Tobu Isesaki Line|WD=Q906598|lt=Tōbu Isesaki Line|1=Tobu Isesaki Line}} ({{OSM route map|start=35.81411,139.80140|goal=35.80924,139.82021|name=Route}}). == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Adachi Historical Society. *History of Adachi Ward*. Meicho Publishing, 1978, pp. 230–232. * Adachi Historical Society (ed.). *Adachi Ward Historical Walks*. Gakuseisha, 1992, pp. 162–166. * {{cite book|title={{ill|Edo meisho zue|en|Edo meisho zue|WD=Q2704637|lt=Edo Meisho Zue|1=Edo meisho zue}}|volume=3|author=Yukio Saitō|publisher=Yūhōdō Shoten|year=1927|chapter=巻之六 開陽之部 正一位大鷲大明神社|pages=521–524|id={{NDLJP|1174157/265}}|ref={{sfnref|Edo Meisho Zue|1927}}}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Ōtori Shrine|ja|鷲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtori Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōtori taisha|en|Ōtori taisha|WD=Q705151|lt=Ōtori taisha|1=Ōtori taisha}} == External Links == * [http://ootori-jinja.or.jp/ Official Ōtori Shrine Website] {{Ōtori Faith}} {{translated page|ja|大鷲神社 (足立区)|version=103732014|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:大鷲神社 (足立区)]] [[ja:大鷲神社]] {{Commons category|Ōtori-jinja (Hanahata, Adachi)}} {{wikidata link|Q11442008}} [[Category:Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Adachi, Tokyo]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]]
